facade_colour: white and turquoise painted enclosed porch over an altered red-brick house-form facade

hcd_data.architectural_style: House-form, Semi-detached, Bay-and-Gable (16 Denison Square) (confirmed – HCD Plan Vol. 2)
hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian and early-twentieth-century architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Each property retains original building features including a cross-gabled roofline, double-height bay, and fenestration pattern. 16 Denison Square has retained its decorative wooden gable brackets while 18 Denison Square has retained its segmentally arched window openings with brick voussoirs and cladding. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.

photo_observations.facade_colour_observed: white and turquoise painted enclosed porch over an altered red-brick house-form facade
photo_observations.facade_condition_notes: front facade is significantly altered with enclosed porch glazing, modern cladding, and repainting; building appears maintained
photo_observations.window_details: enclosed lower porch windows and two modest second-floor windows with replacement frames
photo_observations.door_details: red front door set behind enclosed porch
photo_observations.cornice_details: historic trim largely concealed by later enclosure work
photo_observations.porch_details: full-width enclosed front porch with painted framing
photo_observations.decorative_details: little original detail remains visible on the front facade
photo_observations.alterations_visible: enclosed porch, replacement windows, painted cladding, updated entry door
photo_observations.notes: 2.5-storey Victorian house, red brick lower with teal/green painted upper gable. Orange/wood door visible at upper level. White trim on windows and cornices. Bay window visible on ground floor (left side). Dormered gable at top. Bicycles and items stored on porch.

facade_description: 16 Denison Sq presents a bay-and-gable domestic facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its semi-detached bay-and-gable house-form typology. The facade reads primarily as mixed in a white and turquoise painted enclosed porch over an altered red-brick house-form facade palette, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 2, 2 window bays from lower to upper levels, using mixed, and 1 principal entrance. Character-defining facade elements include bay window, original windows, cornice. HCD note: Each property has value as a House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian and early-twentieth-century architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Each property retains original building features including a cross-gabled roofline, double-height bay, and fenestration pattern. 16 Denison Square has retained its decorative wooden gable brackets while 18 Denison Square has retained its segmentally arched window openings with brick voussoirs and cladding. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.
